title: #sales: 3751234567
preview:
Priya Shah: Acme Infra Team just confirmed they need a fully air-gapped POC next month. Key asks: offline delivery only, no outbound telemetry, upgrade windows once/month, and signed bundles. Can we outline constraints and a delivery pattern?

Acme Infra Team: correct — no egress, jumpboxes allowed, but transfers are via physical media (sneakernet). Also need a way to validate bundle provenance locally.

Ben Carter: We can do offline bundles + manifest signing. Typical flow: build -> sign with Redwood signing key (customer can validate against their supplied pubkey) -> deliver tarball or chunked delta payload. Questions: bundle size cap? and acceptance of needing an HSM/KMS for private key storage?

Acme Infra Team: prefer they keep the pubkey. We cannot hold private keys in their environment. HSM for our side is fine. Bundle size: <10GB preferable. Deltas for model weights would be huge.

Lena Ortiz: For large model blobs we do two options: 1) quantized shards + delta patches (smaller), or 2) offline sync of full model image. We'll need a manifest with checksums and signature metadata (sha256 list) and an SBOM. Also we can produce a "manifest-only" patch that tells the runtime to pull a locally-staged image.

Tom Huang: Pricing/ops note — for POC offline installs we charge a one-time image-burn + transfer fee and a short-term reserved seat cost if they want guaranteed throughput during the POC. If they go to Dedicated later we convert. I'll drop numbers once we confirm bandwidth/ops.

Priya Shah: For upgrade cadence they said once/month patch window. Can we support hotfixes outside window for security-critical CVEs?

Ben Carter: We can support emergency signed hotfixes but process requires: 1) Red team sign-off + signed hotfix bundle 2) customer acceptance steps documented in runbook. Hotfixes will still be delivered via media and must be validated locally.

Acme Infra Team: ok on hotfixes with documented runbook. Also need a verification command we can run offline to check signature and manifest.

Lena Ortiz: Provide this CLI snippet. It runs entirely offline: 

```bash
redwood-verify --bundle /media/signed-bundle.tar.gz --manifest /media/manifest.json --pubkey /opt/acme/keys/redwood.pub
# exits 0 if good, prints sha256 mismatches otherwise
```

Priya Shah: We'll include that in the POC handoff. Also need guidance on cert rotation for long-lived installs (customer wants >12 month lifetime).

Ben Carter: Recommend short-lived certs for signing; for air-gapped customers we support an annual signing key rotation. We can provide a signed-yearly-rotation plan: existing bundles remain verifiable after rotation (we keep historical pubkeys in manifest), and we can provide a utility to import new pubkeys.

Acme Infra Team: How about telemetry? They insist on zero telemetry.

Lena Ortiz: We can provide a build that compiles with telemetry disabled and a hardened runtime (FIPS mode available). Note: some observability features (trace ids, token-level billing) won't run locally without network. We can instead dump logs to local files for manual pickup.

Priya Shah: Ops question — do we need any firewall ports open for licensing activation? They want offline license activation.

Ben Carter: We have an offline license activation flow: signed activation file exchanged via USB. Activation is bound to host fingerprint. No outbound port required.

deploy-bot: bundle-signing: signed-bundle-2026-03-14.tar.gz created, sha256: e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855

Tom Huang: Next steps: Priya to confirm POC dates + whether Acme wants full model image or quantized shards. I will follow up with a POC cost sheet (one-off image transfer + 4-week reserved capacity rate).

Priya Shah: @Acme Infra Team — can you confirm transfer window and whether they'll accept chunked deltas?

Acme Infra Team: transfer window is week of Apr 18. Prefer chunked deltas for daily syncs, but for initial seeding we'll accept a full image on HDD. Also request the SBOM and a simple verification script.

Lena Ortiz: We'll provide SBOM, manifest, verification script, and runbook for monthly upgrades + emergency hotfix procedure. I'll also add notes about FIPS and quantization tradeoffs.

Ben Carter: I'll prepare the signed sample bundle and POC pricing. Expect a follow-up by EOD tomorrow. :thumbsup:

Priya Shah: Thanks team — logging action items: 1) Ben: sample signed bundle + pricing 2) Lena: SBOM + runbook + verification CLI 3) Tom: cost addendum 4) Priya: confirm dates with customer.

Acme Infra Team: thanks all, appreciate the clarity.

title: #sales: 3467890123
preview:
Iris Han: NewCo just sent a terse RFI. They need a one-page capability matrix, short answers on BYOK/HSM behavior, VPC peering, and an estimated POC quote. They want turnaround by EOD. RFI uploaded: s3://redwood-sales/newco/rfi/newco-rfi-2026.pdf

Ben Carter: Suggest a tight 2-day POC: Day 1 ingest + embeddings validation, Day 2 perf runs + handoff. Include small replay script and a minimal eval set.

Sofia Morales: For the matrix I'll use columns: Feature | Hosted | Dedicated | Private | Notes. Rows: customer-managed keys, HSM key import, key export policy, VPC peering, data residency, audit logs, retention. Will note FedRAMP = roadmap.

Iris Han: They listed expected load: ~50 concurrent sessions, ~2k embeddings/day, EU residency requirement. They asked if we allow key export from HSM.

Ben Carter: Security answer: BYOK is supported for Private via PKCS#11 HSMs and for Hosted Enterprise via KMS integrations. We do NOT support private-key export from HSMs. Key rotation and audit logs supported; per-tenant keys at rest.

Legal (triage-bot): NDA + DPA required before ingestion. Recommend POC SOW with 30-day retention cap and deletion verification steps. Pen-test needs 10 business days notice and a scoped test plan.

Sofia Morales: Pricing suggestion: $2.5k platform credits + $750 infra stipend if on-prem kit needed. Optional temporary Dedicated pool listed as add-on in quote. Conversion incentive: 20% credit toward first month if Dedicated commit within 30 days. Finance to confirm.

InfraBot: Tentative POC kickoff scheduled 2026-09-02 09:00 CET. Invite queued to newco-poc@newco.com.

Iris Han: I'll assemble deliverables (capability_matrix_newco_v1.xlsx, rfi_answers_newco.md, poc_quote_newco.pdf) and push to s3://redwood-sales/newco/deliverables/. If legal/finance OK, I'll return the RFI this afternoon.

Ben Carter: Added a short eval curl to the deliverable. Example: curl -X POST https://api.redwood.ai/v1/embeddings -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"model":"rwd-embed-1","input":["sample"]}'

Sofia Morales: Drafts pushed. Legal/finance please review quickly.

Legal (triage-bot): NDA + DPA templates good. Finance please confirm credits/stipend.

Iris Han: Thanks team  going to send once finance confirms.

title: #sales: 3410012345
preview:
Aisha Khan: Quick thread 1025 - BalticBank wants: redacted SOC2 Type II excerpt, copy of ISO 27001 cert, and a signed pen-test attestation for the POC. They also asked if Dedicated POC can use customer-managed KMS keys and whether notarization is necessary. ETA for POC start: next week. Can we confirm process + cost? @Ethan @Omar @Clara
Ethan Park: We can produce redacted SOC2 excerpt and ISO cert copy. For KMS: customer-managed KMS on Dedicated is possible but well need to run a key-wrapping integration and VPC peering. Estimate 72-120 hours to provision.
Ravi Menon: Upload options: presigned EU-only S3 URLs (preferred), secure upload portal (one-time link), or SFTP if they insist. For EU residency we'll route to eu-central-1 bucket. Presigned example:
```
curl -T bank_evidence.zip "https://eu-files.redwood.example/upload?sig=..." -H "x-customer: baltic-123"
```
Aisha Khan: They specifically require EU-only storage and a statement that artifacts will not leave region. Can we confirm data residency retention?
Ethan Park: Yes, evidence stored in eu-central-1 bucket and processed in EU-only staging. Retention window: 30 days by default; can extend on request. Note: cross-region KMS wrapping will contradict EU-only requirement, so we must use EU KMS.
Omar (Finance): Pricing: Dedicated POC with customer-managed KMS (EU HSM) = one-time integration fee €5,500 + 22% uplift on POC reserved monthly pricing for HSM usage and engineering. If notarization requested add €250 for processing/courier. I can formalize quote after we have expected peak QPS.
Clara (Legal): On notarization: we rarely notarize attestation letters unless the customer explicitly insists for legal/regulatory reasons. We can include a signature block for Redwoods authorized signatory; if notarization requested we need 1 additional business day and courier details. Suggested attestation text: "Redwood Inference attests that a third-party penetration test was performed within the mutually agreed scope. This attestation is scope-limited and does not constitute a security warranty." I'll draft and sign.
Ravi Menon: Operational limits for EU uploads: max single file 8GB, multipart supported, presigned URLs default 48 hours expiry. We run sha256 verification and automated PII scanning on ingest. If they prefer SFTP provisioning lead time is 18-36 hours.
Aisha Khan: Customer asks whether we can redact vendor findings (they don't want raw PII). Confirmed: we will supply redacted SOC2 excerpt and high-level scope page from pen-test vendor only. No raw logs.
ingest-bot: Evidence package received: baltic_evidence_341001_1.zip (58MB) -> eu-staging at 2025-04-02T14:03:12Z
Aisha Khan: Evidence uploaded to EU staging. @Clara @Ethan please review and post attestation draft. Can Finance provide a provisional quote?
Ethan Park: I'll post redacted SOC2 excerpt + ISO cert copy in this thread within 24 hrs.
Clara (Legal): Drafting attestation now. Need exact legal entity name & signer name/title for signature block. Please DM.
Omar (Finance): Provisional quote: I'll add to tracker now; need peak QPS numbers to finalize. For now I left placeholder for compute hours.
Aisha Khan: Sending legal entity + signer via DM. Also confirming peak ~200 qps, avg 30 qps. Thanks everyone.

title: #sales: 3380012345
preview:
Aisha Khan: Heads-up — Greenline Capital (EU) wants SOC 2 Type II excerpt + ISO 27001 certificate copy and a signed pen-test attestation for their proof-of-security pack. They also asked whether a Dedicated POC with customer-managed keys (KMS) is feasible and how that affects price. 
Ethan Park: We can hand over a redacted SOC2 summary + ISO cert copy. For KMS: customer-managed keys on Dedicated are doable but requires extra networking and key-wrapping plumbing. Will need 3-5 days for config. @Omar can you flag the pricing impact? 
Ravi Menon: Upload preference? Recommend presigned S3 URLs (short-lived) or transfer via secure upload portal (we can provide one-time links). SFTP is possible but slower to stand up. Quick sample: 
```
# client uploads
curl -T evidence.tar.gz "https://files.redwood.example/upload?sig=..." -H "x-client-id: GL123"
```
Aisha Khan: They specifically asked for EU-only hosting for the evidence and for logs to stay in region. Can we confirm where uploads land? 
Ethan Park: Yes — for EU customers we route evidence to our eu-west-1 bucket (data residency). Presigned URLs point to eu bucket. Note: cross-region KMS adds complexity. 
Omar (Finance): Pricing note: Dedicated POC with customer-managed KMS = one-time setup fee (~$6k) + 25% uplift on monthly reserved for the POC run (covers HSM usage and engineering time). I'll prepare a quote; need expected vCPU/GPU hours or peak qps. 
Clara (Legal): For the attestation letter, suggest limiting language: "Redwood Inference confirms coordination of a third-party penetration test within the documented scope and provides this attestation for the POC. This attestation is not a warranty of security." I can draft the final wording and sign. 
Aisha Khan: They only want the attestation + high-level scope, not full vendor report. Confirmed. Also they asked whether artifact redaction is acceptable (they expect redacted vendor findings). 
Ravi Menon: Operational details: max single file 10GB for eu uploads, multipart supported. Presigned URLs expire in 48 hours by default. We verify sha256 on ingest and scan for PII patterns before passing to legal. SFTP provisioning takes ~18-24 hours. 
upload-bot: Evidence upload received: greenline_evidence_338001.tar.gz (44MB) -> eu-staging at 2025-03-12T09:08:22Z
Aisha Khan: Uploaded to staging. @Ethan @Clara please review attestation draft + SOC2 excerpt. 
Ethan Park: I'll assemble the redacted SOC2 summary and ISO cert copy tonight and post here. ETA: 24 hrs. 
Clara (Legal): I'll prepare the attestation template and will need the exact legal entity name plus signatory name/title for the signature block. Also confirm if customer needs notarization. 
Omar (Finance): Quote in progress. Aisha — what's the expected POC throughput? Put numbers in tracker and I'll finalize. 
Aisha Khan: Sending legal entity + signatory info now via DM. Throughput: estimated 500 qps peak for 2 days, avg 50 qps. 
Ethan Park: Noted. That throughput uses Dedicated minimal cluster; will also recommend prefix caching to cut cost. 
Clara (Legal): Draft posted in thread. Small edit: add clause about scope-limited attestation and retention period for evidence (we hold on staging for 30 days). 
Aisha Khan: Thanks. Once Clara signs, I'll share with customer and confirm upload channel. :white_check_mark:

title: #sales: 3167890123
preview:
Priya Rao: Heads up — AstraBridge (large healthcare insurer) just escalated. CFO wants a 1pm PT exec call Tuesday to sign off POC, but they flagged two blockers: KMS BYO + SOC2 evidence. Need quick alignment.
Alex Chen: putting together a short runbook. Do we have a current BYO-KMS playbook for Private deploy?
Mateo Alvarez: We have a draft for VPC + BYO-KMS but it assumes Private deployment and on-prem HSM. For Dedicated we'd need legal ok for key escrow.
Sarah Li: I can lead the call demo and cover the latency/cost numbers. Need answers on KMS timeline and a list of SOC2 artifacts they expect.
Rahul Patel: Can you share the questionnaire? I haven't seen it yet. If they want our SOC2 report, we can provide the latest certified PDF. For BYO-KMS we need threat model + attestation from their KMS provider.
Priya Rao: attaching their note from yesterday: "CFO: cannot proceed w/o customer-managed keys for PHI. Security to confirm." They also want a discounted committed rate if we run Dedicated for the POC.
Alex Chen: quick technical note: BYO-KMS path requires 1) network peering + egress allowlist, 2) KMS key wrapping config, 3) operator checklist for key rotation. I can draft the minimal config snippet for the call.
Alex Chen: ```
# minimal KMS binding example
kms_provider: aurakey
key_id: projects/astro/locations/global/keyRings/poc-key
allowlist_ips:
  - 35.203.12.45
  - 34.120.8.16
```
Mateo Alvarez: Platform-wise, we can spin a Private nodepool in their AWS account in ~3 business days if they grant infra role. Dedicated on Redwood side is faster but we can't offer BYO-KMS there without legal changes.
Jordan Bell: Legal here — for escrow/attestation we need their KMS vendor contract copy and ATO details. Also they asked for a short addendum for holdbacks if PHI handling is involved. I can draft a one-pager.
Sarah Li: Pricing ask — CFO pushing for 25% off committed 3-month POC on Dedicated. I need approval or counter to propose on the call.
Priya Rao: Exec ask: can an SVP-level person join the 1pm PT call to reassure them about roadmap + commitments? They said it's a make-or-break signoff.
Rahul Patel: I can put together the SOC2 pack and an email summary of BYO risk mitigations. Will need Ops to confirm we've no data exfil flags.
Alex Chen: @Mateo can you confirm whether we can enable KMS binding in Dedicated as an exception? If yes, slack legal + infra for emergency exception process.
Mateo Alvarez: Not without a contract amendment. I'll escalate to eng-platform lead. ETA for response: EOD today.
Jordan Bell: I'll prepare the addendum draft and circulate for review by noon tomorrow. If someone drops the CFO a quick note that legal is prepping it, that buys time.
OpsBot: [reminder] calendar invite created: "AstraBridge exec POC signoff" Tue 13:00 PT — attendees: Priya Rao, Alex Chen, Sarah Li, Mateo Alvarez, Jordan Bell, Rahul Patel.
Priya Rao: Thanks everyone. Action items: Alex - draft KMS snippet + one-slide risk mitigation (today). Mateo - escalate exception (EOD). Jordan - addendum draft (tomorrow noon). preview:
Ava Chen: Morning — async check for AuroraCare pilot. Quick status: demo on Wed went well, they want Dedicated for compliance. What I did: sent follow-up and high-level infra ask to SEs. Blockers: customer asked for BYOK + HSM integration details and timeline. Today: gathering answers from security and SEs, prepping draft contract clause for BYOK.

Mike Patel: Got it. I pushed a short runbook for Dedicated onboarding to the repo: https://internal.redwood/handbooks/dedicated-onboarding — see section 3 for key network items. I can extract the HSM integration bits.

Sarah Ortiz: FYI the AuroraCare legal team also asked for SOC2 evidence and key rotation policy. I can pull our rotation docs. Blocker for me is waiting on KMS scope from Security.

Josh Nguyen: SE note — the Dedicated cluster requires a small config change to allow external KMS callbacks. Example snippet I shared with the pilot: `k8s/secret-provider: enabled: true
kms: endpoint: https://kms.customer.example` They want confirmation we support bring-your-own-key (BYOK) via HSM. Short answer: yes, but need product + ops approval for timeline.

Rebecca Li: I pinged SecurityOps this morning. SecurityOps, can you confirm if we officially support HSM-backed BYOK in Private today, and any constraints (latency, region limits)? Also need sample SLA wording for the contract.

SecurityOps: Short answer — we support customer-managed KMS with external HSM via key-wrap and VPC peering patterns. Constraints: only in us-west-2 and eu-west-1 for now, and we require a scheduled key-rotation process. I'll DM the exact checklist. For SLA language, use: "Customer-managed keys will be honored; Redwood is not liable for compromise of customer KMS." (legal can refine).

Mike Patel: Thanks. Can you drop the checklist link here when you have it? Also -- have we validated egress rules? AuroraCare plans to run inference on long sequences so we might need higher quota.

SecurityOps: Checklist uploaded: https://internal.redwood/security/byok-checklist — includes egress/latency notes and firewall template. Quotas: request opens a ticket to Infra with expected RPS and sequence lengths.

Ava Chen: Good, I'm updating the customer doc and will include the checklist link. Quick ask: legal needs a sample paragraph for indemnity related to key custody. Who owns drafting that?

Sarah Ortiz: I can draft a first pass and loop in Legal. Will tag Legal in the contract PR.

Josh Nguyen: SE follow-up: I raised an engineering spike to estimate perf impact for HSM KMS calls (internal ticket INFR-984). If we need synchronous KMS calls each request, latency could add ~30–50ms per call; caching tokens reduces that. Blocker: need a clear cache TTL policy from SecurityOps.

SecurityOps: Recommend cache TTL 15m for wrapped tokens, with forced rotation on role change. I'll add to the checklist.

Ava Chen: Perfect. Next steps for the thread: 1) Sarah to draft indemnity paragraph, 2) SecurityOps to post checklist and TTL (done), 3) Josh to update INFR-984 with perf estimate, 4) Mike to prepare quota request for Infra, 5) I'll send updated customer note. title: #sales: 2141598765
preview:
Ava Chen: Quick kickoff note — I put together a compact presales playbook for the Acme pilot. Need alignment on the eval 'gradebook' (golden set size, scoring weights, regression gates), security questionnaire path, and any pricing constraints that would block the PoC. TL;DR: 2-week test window, can we confirm?

Raj Patel: 2-week window sounds fine. Pricing Q: if they want Dedicated for predictable latency, do we quote committed monthly + overage, or a flat pilot price? Need guidance for commercial.

Tess Morgan: For pilots we usually offer a short-term reserved bundle (4 weeks of reduced commit) + hourly overage. But for enterprise buyers we can do a fixed pilot fee that covers reserved capacity and support. I can draft numbers once we lock expected qps and model size.

Miguel Santos: Ops question — what throughput/latency SLO are we using as a success metric? Also need the dataset footprint to size dedicated GPUs.

Tess Morgan: Proposed success criteria (suggested):
- Latency: p95 generation <= 250ms for typical prompts (seq len ~512)
- Quality: mean human score >= 4.0/5 on golden set (see scoring below)
- Recall / factualness: automatic recall@5 >= 0.88 on retrieval tasks
- Cost guardrail: pilot average token cost <= $0.012/token (configurable)

Jordan Lee: For gradebook we recommend: 150 golden examples (50 intent tests, 50 extraction/factual, 50 multi-turn flows). Hold out 60 examples for regression gates. Seed set for prompting/temperature tuning = 300 examples.

Ava Chen: That distribution sounds good. We need a simple scoring rubric so product owners can grade quickly. Jordan, can you paste a compact example?

Jordan Lee: Sure — example scoring rubric (weights):
```
{ "intent_accuracy": 0.35, "extraction_f1": 0.35, "fluency": 0.15, "safety": 0.15 }
```
Manuals: intent -> binary correct/incorrect, extraction -> F1 per field, fluency -> 1-5 Likert, safety -> 1-5 Likert (policy hits = 0). Aggregate to weighted mean.

Raj Patel: For commercial ask — if they request model pinning + audit logs (enterprise), we add the Private deployment add-on fee. Will surface numbers after Tess drafts the pilot fee.

Miguel Santos: Dataset/infra notes: if golden set ~150 examples (avg 800 tokens), seed 300, regressions 60, we need ~5–10GB of storage for eval artifacts and KV caches for repeated runs. Dedicated: 1 A100 equivalent should be enough for 100 qps at this config.

Ava Chen: On regression gates: propose simple rule — if weighted mean drops by >5% vs baseline on holdout, fail and rollback. Also block release if any safety metric drops by >1 point. Thoughts?

Jordan Lee: Agree. Also add automation: run reg tests nightly during pilot. If regression triggered, open an incident in #release-war-room and send alerts to the deal team.

Tess Morgan: Prompt-set design: aim for concise templates + templated metadata per example: {scenario, prompt_template, expected_entities, accept_variants}. Example prompt snippet below: 
```
Prompt: "Summarize the customer's request and list 3 action items."
Expected: summary (1-2 sentences), actions: list of 3 bullets
```
We should capture 2 golden prompts per flow to cover variance.

Ava Chen: Security questionnaire: LegalBot uploaded a filled SQA draft (answers + outstanding asks). Link: https://rwd.internal/sales/acme/sqa-draft (internal)

LegalBot: New SQA draft uploaded by Legal: https://rwd.internal/sales/acme/sqa-draft — outstanding: data residency clarification, encryption-at-rest key owner, and SOC attestation docs.

Raj Patel: Those are typical asks. We can provide KMS integration details and our SOC snapshot. Need Legal to confirm export controls.

Miguel Santos: Blocker note: customer asked for on-prem evaluation possibility. That adds 3 weeks to timeline for packaging + security review. Flagging to the deal team.

Ava Chen: Updated timeline proposal:
- Week 0: finalize gradebook + SQA answers + commercial terms
- Week 1: ingest golden set + seed set, run baseline evaluation, smoke test infra
- Week 2: closed-loop refinement (prompt tuning), nightly regression runs, demo to stakeholders at end of week
If on-prem is required, shift to 5-week plan.

Jordan Lee: Action items (assign):
- Jordan: prepare gradebook templates + tooling export (csv + json) — due EOD tomorrow
- Tess: draft pilot fee + Dedicated vs pilot flat fee options — due +48h
- Miguel: confirm infra sizing + KMS integration steps — due +48h
- Raj: confirm commercial caveats around on-prem and add-on language — due +72h
- LegalBot / Legal: finalize SQA answers and SOC docs — due +72h

Ava Chen: I'll consolidate into a one-page playbook for the exec sponsor after we get these items. Thanks team. :thumbsup:

Tess Morgan: ++ — will ping finance for the draft numbers.

Miguel Santos: Noted. I'll also add a quick runbook for nightly regression automation.

Raj Patel: I'll prep contract language for pilot terms and on-prem clause.

Jordan Lee: uploading gradebook template now: https://rwd.internal/sales/acme/gradebook.csv

Ava Chen: Perfect — closing thread for now, will reopen if new blockers. :sparkles:

title: #sales: 1971112223
preview:
Ophelia Santos: Heads-up — running into a tricky ask from Acme Corp. They want a private on‑prem deployment (air‑gapped option) with N+1 standby nodes, KMS/HSM integration, and a 3yr commit. Customer requests: waive platform fee for the POC (30 days), reduce support tier to Standard after year 1, and a capped surcharge for standby capacity. Can deal desk/finance sign off on any of this?
Ben Carter: Thanks — a few pieces to unpack. Do we know expected steady RPS and peak concurrency? surcharge sizing depends on idle standby GPU count. :thinking_face:
Ophelia Santos: They're estimating 5 RPS steady, peaks ~40 RPS during batch windows. Standby is 2 extra A100s pinned in-region.
Ravi Patel: From an engineering perspective, standby nodes mean reserved capacity billed as "hot reserve". We can autoscale but for air-gapped they want manual failover so reserve is simpler. That typically maps to a 25–35% uplift on top of base infra monthly.
Ben Carter: Agreed. For a 3yr commit we can provide a commit discount on the infra and a deferred platform fee schedule. But platform fee waiver for the POC is negotiable only if AE covers rollout risk via a conditional credit.
Sam Ortega: Finance: we can approve a 30‑day platform fee credit for proof-of-value if contract has a go/no-go milestone and a repayment clause if they cancel post-POC. Need ARR commitment + signature.
Ophelia Santos: Noted. They want the credit up front — how to phrase repayment clause?
Ben Carter: Suggested language: "If customer terminates within 45 days after POC without conversion, customer agrees to repay the POC credit unless termination is due to Provider breach or failed security/compliance gating." Keep it simple.

preview:
Ava Chen: FYI running into a pricing blocker with NovaTec — POC went well, they're asking for a 40% off annual committed Dedicated plan to match a competitor. Need deal desk approval asap, deadline EOD Wed.
Ravi Patel: 40% seems high. What's their expected commit $/mo and term?
Ava Chen: They're looking at 250k/year committed (reserved GPU capacity), 12mo. Reported competitor offered aggressive bundled infra + support.
Marta Lopez: Send the customer profile and win rationale. We can consider a one-off but need Finance + Product ok. @Dan Kim can you weigh in on runway impact?
Dan Kim: 40% on 250k = large gap. We could entertain up to 25% with a 12mo fixed commit + strict MMR/exit clauses. Need margin calc and support lift estimate.
Julian Ortiz: Technically we can scope a lighter support bundle for POC-to-prod migration to reduce cost. Proposal: 25% off base + $12k/year for priority on-call.
Ava Chen: Customer insists on 35% to make internal approval. They have legal concerns about data residency too — want Private deployment (VPC) included. That adds infra ops cost.
Marta Lopez: @Legal-bot can you confirm any additional contractual exposure for VPC in this region?
Legal-bot: VPC add-on requires SOC2 addendum + KMS alignment. No material change to liability, but provisioning SLA needs to be defined.
Ravi Patel: Ok — looks like two levers: discount % and scope (VPC + support). Recommend: counteroffer 30% off for 12mo commit if they accept limited VPC (no on-prem, region EU-west only) + $15k one-time setup.
Dan Kim: 30% still dents margin but acceptable if we get prepayment or invoice schedule to improve cash flow. Need CFO sign-off for >28% discounts.
Marta Lopez: I can approve up to 28%. For anything above that, escalate to CFO. @Ava can you ask customer to take prepay or a 2-quarter upfront?
Ava Chen: I'll push for 2-quarter upfront and the EU-west constraint. If they push back, ask for 35% with a 24mo commit instead?
Julian Ortiz: 24mo reduces churn risk and amortizes setup. From infra POV fine. Recommend adding a renewal price floor.
Ava Chen: Great. I'll present two options: A) 28% off, 12mo, 2-quarters upfront, EU-west VPC + $15k setup; B) 35% off, 24mo, 1-quarter upfront, EU-west VPC + $5k setup discount.
Marta Lopez: Sounds good. Document both proposals in the SOW and include fallback clause if infra costs exceed X. I'll standby for final numbers to route to CFO.
Ravi Patel: Ping me when you have final SOW, I'll prepare the finance impact note.
Ava Chen: Will do. Thanks all — target to have SOW by tomorrow morning. :pray:
Dan Kim: Copy — if it hits >28% I'll loop in CFO.
Marta Lopez: Tagged for approval flow. :white_check_mark:
Ava Chen: Closing thread for now, will update after customer call.

title: #sales: 1893456789
preview:
Ava Chen: Heads up — Acme Health wants to procure via AWS Private Offers. Procurement team is saying invoice needs to be issued to the legal parent 'Acme Global, Inc' not the subsidiary. They're asking for the PO to reference our AWS offer SKU.
Ravi Patel: That’s common. The tricky part: AWS will bill through the marketplace seller account, which shows as 'Redwood Inference, Inc' on the AWS invoice. Procurement wants the P.O. to match the invoice entity.
Ava Chen: Right — Acme procurement said they can't process if the billing entity on the invoice != PO entity. They asked if we can route invoice to Acme Global.
Jordan Lee (Deal Desk): @Ava Chen do we have precedent? We usually: 1) recommend Private Offer with consolidated billing mapping, or 2) accept a PO to the marketplace entity and provide an intercompany addendum.
Megan Ortiz (Finance): +1. We need paper trail for revenue recognition. If Acme insists on invoice naming, we either need to (a) create a custom invoice to Acme Global (manual), or (b) ask procurement for an account alias/linked billing account on their side.
Ravi Patel: Quick checklist I shared with customers before:
```
- Is the AWS Marketplace account under the legal entity that issued the PO?
- Can the buyer add a billing alias or linked account to make invoices land under the parent?
- If not, will their AP accept an addendum referencing the marketplace invoice? 
```
Ava Chen: Acme says they won't accept addendum. They want the invoice header to match PO exactly.
Jordan Lee (Deal Desk): Okay — deal desk options: we can approve a one-time exception to invoice to Acme Global, but Finance needs full justification and legal signoff. That triggers a manual billing flow and a 2–3 week lead time.
Megan Ortiz (Finance): Manual invoice is possible, but we flag this as a non-standard procurement risk. Also need to confirm implications for our AWS payout reconciliation — marketplace fees still apply.
procurement-bot: Reminder: private-offer-sku: rwd-inf-private-xyz will be the committed SKU. AWS collects the fee on the marketplace invoice; seller payout uses that SKU.
Ravi Patel: Another option: convert the deal to Dedicated (reserved capacity) — then Redwood invoices directly to customer entity (no marketplace). But that changes pricing/commit and timeline.
Ava Chen: Customer prefers to use marketplace credits they have. They want commerce to go through AWS.
Jordan Lee (Deal Desk): If they insist, and they'd like to use marketplace credits, the path is: 1) create AWS Private Offer 2) request buyer billing alias/linked account 3) if alias not possible, escalate to legal to approve a manual invoice to parent and sign a Waiver & Billing Addendum.
Megan Ortiz (Finance): To expedite: Ava — can you get procurement to confirm the exact name & tax ID they require on the invoice? We need: legal name, billing address, and EIN/VAT.
Ava Chen: Sent email to procurement asking for exact invoice header and whether they'll accept a PDF copy of the AWS invoice plus our supplemental invoice.
Ravi Patel: FYI — if procurement's lawyers require our company to be the payee on the invoice, they might also ask for the marketplace percentage to be shown separately. AWS invoices won't show our margin line item. That may be the blocker.
Jordan Lee (Deal Desk): Deal desk can approve a special term: "Supplier will issue a supplemental commercial invoice to buyer entity showing Redwood charges net of marketplace fees." Needs legal/finance signoff.
Megan Ortiz (Finance): If we do a supplemental invoice, we must avoid double-billing. We'll include statement: "This invoice is for commercial reconciliation only; AWS invoice is the legal tax invoice." Need procurement to accept that language.
Ava Chen: Procurement replied — they can accept supplemental invoice if AWS invoice references the Private Offer ID and shows charge amounts. They still require the PO to be made out to Acme Global.
Ravi Patel: Action items: Ava to collect exact header + PO PDF; Jordan to draft the supplemental invoice language; Megan to estimate manual billing timeline and any fee impact.
Ava Chen: Sending PO now. PO number: PO-ACME-2026-314. They put legal name: Acme Global, Inc; EIN: 12-3456789; billing addr provided.
Jordan Lee (Deal Desk): Drafted wording (first pass):
```
Redwood Inference, Inc. (Seller) will provide a supplemental commercial invoice to Acme Global, Inc. for reconciliation purposes. The AWS Marketplace invoice issued by Amazon Web Services, Inc. shall remain the legal tax invoice. The supplemental invoice is not a substitute legal invoice and is provided solely to assist Buyer AP in matching charges to the PO. Private Offer ID: 0abcd-priv-offer-1234.
```
Megan Ortiz (Finance): That looks ok. Add this clause: "Seller confirms it will not seek duplicate payment for amounts invoiced by AWS."
Jordan Lee (Deal Desk): Added.
Ava Chen: Procurement accepts the supplemental language but wants a formal statement on our letterhead and a contact in Finance for remittance questions.
Megan Ortiz (Finance): I'll be the contact. I can sign the billing addendum. Reminder: we should mark this as an exception in the CRM and flag for future deals — procurement note for Acme Global.
Ravi Patel: One more note — because they use AWS credits, billing timing could mismatch. Recommend adding a line: "If buyer’s AWS credits cover the charges, the supplemental invoice will show $0 due."
Jordan Lee (Deal Desk): Approved by deal desk subject to legal signoff. Legal loop required but low risk.
Ava Chen: Great — I'll update the opportunity and send the addendum + signed supplemental invoice template on company letterhead.
Megan Ortiz (Finance): I’ll estimate 5 business days for manual invoice generation and one more for legal signature. Expect payout timing shift by 7–10 days vs standard marketplace.
Ava Chen: Thanks everyone — resolving. :tada:
Procurement-bot: Ticket created: BILLING-4621 (manual-invoice-acme).

- reply_suggest_long_v1: "Write a detailed reply (4-6 sentences) with suggested troubleshooting steps."

title: #sales: 1839123456
preview:
Emily Harper: Heads-up — Fathom Labs (enterprise AI analytics) asked for a hosted pilot concession. They want a temporary per-token rate for embeddings due to very high volume.
Raj Singh: How big is "very high"? ballpark numbers pls.
Emily Harper: Rough estimate from kickoff: 50M embeddings over 90 days. Each embedding ~ 512 tokens -> ~25.6B tokens.
Mateo Alvarez: whoa that's nontrivial for hosted. Are they insisting on hosted or open to Dedicated?
Emily Harper: They prefer hosted for speed of iteration. Dedicated would be heavier ops + procurement, they said pilot needs to be low friction.
Raj Singh: What's our standard hosted embedding rate today?
Priya Chen: Standard: $0.10 per 1K tokens for embeddings (public rate card). Min monthly commit kicks in at $5k for pilot accounts by policy.
Emily Harper: Their ask: waive the $5k min for the 90-day pilot and give a one-time discounted per-token rate of $0.03 per 1K tokens for embeddings only.
Mateo Alvarez: Technical note: at 25.6B tokens, cost at standard would be ~$2,560 (if $0.10 per 1K -> $0.00010 per token -> 25.6B * 0.0001 = $2.56M) wait double-check math.
Raj Singh: quick calc: $0.10/1K = $0.0001/token. 25.6B tokens * $0.0001 = $2,560,000. yep.
Priya Chen: That volume would normally be handled as a committed deal or Dedicated. The ask to drop to $0.03/1K would be a 70% discount and materially below acceptable floors.
Raj Singh: Can we offer a structured alternative? e.g., temporary tiering: first 100M tokens at $0.03/1K, remaining at $0.08/1K, and require a $50k pilot deposit (refundable / creditable)?
Priya Chen: Finance guidance: we can approve up to a 25% discount off list without VP sign-off for pilots. Anything deeper needs Deal Desk + revenue ops + VP approval. Also we cannot waive min entirely for this scale without a refundable deposit or commitment.
Emily Harper: Fathom is pushing back on deposit. They want to avoid upfront money for procurement reasons. Any levers?
Mateo Alvarez: We can propose usage caps + automatic throttles to limit spend risk, and prefix KV caching to substantially reduce token usage for embeddings in practice. But that doesn't replace economics.
Raj Singh: I'll open a Deal Desk request. Can you send a concise ask format? copy/paste for the form: customer, pilot length, est tokens, requested rate, requested waive min, impact to revenue, rep rec.
Emily Harper: copy: "Fathom Labs — 90d pilot — est 25.6B tokens — requested embeddings rate $0.03/1K — waive $5k min — rep rec: offer tiered concession: $0.03/1K first 200M tokens, $0.07/1K next 1B, $0.09/1K thereafter + $50k refundable pilot deposit or auto-convert to 3mo commit at $0.07/1K."
Raj Singh: perfect. submitting now. @deal-desk-bot can you create a thread and route to VP RevOps?
deal-desk-bot: Request created — DD-38947. Routing to @Priya Chen and @VP-RevOps. Please attach the cost model and customer LOE.
Priya Chen: I'll review DD-38947. Preliminary: we like the tiered approach + refundable deposit. If customer absolutely refuses deposit, we could accept a smaller deposit but then need executive approval and legal to sign an Addendum limiting liability.
Emily Harper: I'll push the tiered + refundable deposit option with Fathom and ask if they will auto-convert to a short commit instead of deposit.
Mateo Alvarez: FYI — if they convert to a committed volume we can offer service-level routing and pre-warmed caches to keep latency predictable. Mention that to them.
Raj Singh: Thanks all. I'll follow up after DD decisions. :thumbsup:

title: #sales: 1805234011
preview:
Aisha Khan: Hey team — Acme legal sent a CAIQ and a few follow-ups: they want explicit shared-responsibility language for KMS/encryption + who owns logs in Dedicated vs Private. Also they want a POC quote: 2-week Dedicated footprint ~4x A100 equivalent. Can security + solutions confirm wording and a ballpark price?
Ben Lee: Quick answers: for Hosted we manage KMS (customer keys optional via BYOK), for Dedicated the customer can bring KMS in a VPC-private deployment or we can manage keys with a contractual boundary. Logging: we retain platform/system logs; customer is responsible for application-level logs in Private.
Marta Ruiz: to add — for Private (on-prem/VPC) we provide the control plane but customer can opt to run their own KMS/HSM. Language we usually use: "Customer-managed keys for Private deployments; Redwood-managed keys for Hosted unless BYOK is configured." Want the CAIQ phrased to match SIG expectations.
Connor O'Neill: POC pricing: 2-week Dedicated with 4xA100 equiv — rough estimate: $Xk/week + one-time setup. I need to confirm burst policy and whether they need reserved capacity. Can we confirm SLOs they'll want for the POC (latency p95/throughput)?
Aisha Khan: They asked for baseline SLA = p95 < 250ms for single-request generation and 1000 reqs/min. Also want canary rollback behavior documented.
Ethan Park: audit/log details: platform audit logs (auth, infra changes, model deployments) we retain 90 days by default in Hosted; Dedicated contracts can extend retention or forward to customer SIEM via syslog/Kinesis. App logs = customer.
Ben Lee: For the CAIQ controls, propose this mapping (short):
```
CCM Control      | Responsibility
-----------------|-----------------------
Encryption (E-02)| Shared: Customer keys for Private; Redwood-managed for Hosted
Logging (L-05)   | Shared: Platform logs = Redwood; App logs = Customer
Key Mgmt (K-01)  | Customer for BYOK; Redwood for managed keys
Access (A-03)    | Redwood for platform admin; Customer for their users
```
Marta Ruiz: here's a suggested CAIQ answer snippet we can paste in: "Redwood Inference documents shared responsibility per deployment model. For Private/VPC/on-prem customers, key management and application logs are controlled by the customer unless otherwise contracted. For Hosted/Dedicated we provide platform-managed key options and platform audit logs; customers may configure BYOK and external log forwarding. Specific retention, rotation, and access controls are documented per contract."
Connor O'Neill: I'll drop that into the CAIQ fields. For the pricing, guesstimate: $28,000/week for dedicated 4xA100 equivalence (includes infra + baseline support) -> 2-week POC = $56k + onboarding (one-time $4k). Mark as provisional.
Aisha Khan: can we mark the pricing as provisional and include a footnote about potential discounts for committed usage? Also need a note for the customer that if they choose BYOK we may need an initial key-exchange step and HSM attestation.
Ben Lee: Add this security note for CAIQ: "If BYOK/HSM is selected, Redwood will coordinate attestation and provide guidance; cryptographic operations may be performed in-customer HSM for Private deployments. Redwood performs key rotation for managed keys and logs key operations in platform audit records."

Ben Lee: ack. I'll do a quick pass this afternoon.
Marta Ruiz: ack, will prepare a one-paragraph contract addendum for BYOK + log forwarding.
Connor O'Neill: will update CAIQ fields and post the final JSON here before sending.
Aisha Khan: thanks all — closing thread once uploaded to go/redwood/poc/ACME-221.

title: #sales: 1774205123
preview:
Ava Lin: FYI — ran into a routing gap for POCs that include multimonth commits + non-standard discounts. CPQ currently sends these to Sales Ops, but we need Deal Desk review for pricing exceptions > 15% or any legal special terms.
Ben Carter: Can you paste the CPQ field that's triggering the current route?
Ava Lin: It's this right now: `Special_Pricing_Flag__c` (boolean) + `Discount_Percent__c`. routing rule only checks flag, not commit length.
Priya Sharma: That explains it. We need a compound rule: if Commit_Months__c >= 6 OR Discount_Percent__c > 15 -> route to Deal Desk. Otherwise standard Approval Queue.
Marcus Lee: +1. Also surface `Security_Questionnaire_Required__c` — if true, force Deal Desk + Security review. Some enterprise POCs include an SSA.
Ben Carter: Agree. Proposal for new mapping:```salesforce
CASE(
  WHEN Commit_Months__c >= 6 THEN 'deal-desk'
  WHEN Discount_Percent__c > 15 THEN 'deal-desk'
  WHEN Security_Questionnaire_Required__c = TRUE THEN 'deal-desk-security'
  ELSE 'standard-approval'
)
```
Ava Lin: Nice. Two operational asks: 1) update CPQ price rule and 2) add a routing audit log field `Deal_Route_Reason__c` so downstream ops can troubleshoot.

title: #sales: 1770219944
preview:
Jordan Blake: Need quick escalation + approval on a discount/credit exception for an enterprise deal. @Sofia Mendes @Michael Grant @Laura Bennett @Rishi Malhotra looping you in.

Jordan Blake: Account: ZenChat (procurement stage). They’re asking for an exception to our standard usage pricing for Hosted API.
- Term: 12 months
- Commit: $300k annual usage commit (prepay)
- Ask: 22% discount off list for overage rate + $30k one-time credits to cover ramp
- Rationale: they’re spooked by token-metering changes/news + want “price protection” while they validate cost.
- Close date: end of next week. Redlines on usage definition are still open.

Maya Alvarez: +1 context: ZenChat wants credits applied to “any usage including embeddings + rerank.” Also asked if credits can extend beyond term if unused (I said no, pending approval).

Soojin Lee: Flagging: this overlaps w/ the Q1 packaging experiment window. We should be careful to not create a one-off that breaks guardrails / creates a new anchor.

Chris Osei: Do we know if they’re in any experiment cohort right now (Variant B)? If yes, we should exclude from experiment readouts or tag as “manual exception.”

Maya Alvarez: They’re sales-led, not self-serve. I don’t think they’re in Variant B, but I’ll confirm in HubSpot.

Jordan Blake: Here’s the HubSpot link: https://hubspot.redwoodinference.internal/company/zenchat (internal). Notes are in “Pricing & Legal.”

Rishi Malhotra: Finance questions:
1) What’s the expected monthly burn profile? (helps margin / revrec timing)
2) Any Dedicated component or purely Hosted API?
3) Is the $30k credit a true “make-good” or marketing incentive? (accounting treatment)

Maya Alvarez: Burn: they’re projecting ~20–25M tokens/day after month 2, plus embeddings batches for search. No Dedicated this year, purely Hosted API.

Laura Bennett: 22% off list + $30k credits on a $300k commit is effectively >30% concession depending on burn. What’s the competitive pressure? Are we matching someone?

Jordan Blake: Competitive: they’re comparing to “build on own GPUs” + another inference vendor offering 25% off + big credits, but no Private deployment option. They care about audit logs + retention + VPC later.

Emily Stone: RevRec angle: if this is a prepay commit, credits that can be applied to the same performance obligation need to be clearly defined in the order form (and not open-ended). Also: no “credits roll over past term” unless we want a mess.

Sofia Mendes: Legal questions:
- Are we offering any special dispute/refund language tied to metering changes?
- Is this a standard order form or are we letting them customize usage definitions?
If they’re asking for “price protection,” procurement often tries to sneak in retroactive adjustments.

Michael Grant: +1. If we approve anything, we should keep it in the commercial construct (discount + capped credits), not “we’ll re-rate invoices if meter definitions change.” That becomes an auditability nightmare.

Nikhil Sharma: Product note: with the metering hardening initiative, we can point to “billed_tokens” + cache-hit attribution improvements, but we should avoid contractually guaranteeing a specific token-counting implementation beyond the definitions.

Jordan Blake: They did ask for: “If token counting changes materially, customer can dispute and receive retroactive credits.” I told them we can discuss a bounded dispute window + standard process, but not open-ended retro.

Sofia Mendes: That’s the right answer. We can offer our standard dispute window + audit trail. No retroactive “meter change true-up” language.

Chris Osei: For experiment hygiene, please add a note in HubSpot: “manual pricing exception approved” + tag for exclusion. If we don’t, this will pollute conversion + ARPU.

Maya Alvarez: Will do.

Rishi Malhotra: Proposed Finance counter (to fit guardrails):
- Discount: cap at 15% off list overage rate
- Credits: up to $15k, must be used within first 90 days, applies to Hosted API only (exclude Dedicated, if added later)
- No extension, no cash value
This keeps effective concession closer to ~20% max.

Laura Bennett: Agree with Rishi’s structure. Jordan/Maya—does that still close?

Jordan Blake: Might be tight. They explicitly asked for 20%+ to “feel safe.” Can we flex on one dimension?

Emily Stone: If we flex, I’d rather flex discount than credits. Credits are harder operationally (invoice application timing, customer confusion, support tickets).

Michael Grant: +1. Credits also get negotiated endlessly. Discount is cleaner if the usage definition is tight.

Sofia Mendes: Also if we do credits, we must specify: applies to “Usage Charges” only, not taxes/fees, and only after invoice issuance. And we need a clear expiration date.

Jordan Blake: Ok. How about:
- 18% discount on overage
- $10k credits, expiring end of month 3
- $300k prepay commit unchanged

Rishi Malhotra: 18% + $10k is acceptable from margin perspective given no Dedicated and expected ramp. But I want confirmation they won’t push for retro true-ups tied to metering.

Sofia Mendes: I can take the redline. We’ll keep standard usage definition language + standard dispute window. Any “meter change” clause gets struck.

Michael Grant: Add: “Redwood may update its measurement systems; billing will be based on the Usage Metrics as defined herein.” Plus dispute window (e.g., 30 days) and sole remedy as credit on a future invoice, at our discretion.

Laura Bennett: Approving the 18% + $10k credits (3-month expiry) contingent on:
1) No retroactive re-rating clause
2) Credits do not extend beyond term / no cash value
3) ZenChat excluded/tagged from pricing experiment analysis

Jordan Blake: :thumbsup: Thanks. @Sofia Mendes / @Michael Grant can you confirm once the order form language is clean? We’ll hold the line on the “meter change” ask.

Sofia Mendes: Yep. I’ll update the redline today and send to the deal room. Will loop @Emily Stone before we send.

Maya Alvarez: Updating HubSpot now with the approved structure + experiment exclusion tag.

Chris Osei: 🙏 please also note “approval source: Laura + Rishi + Legal” so we can audit later.

Jordan Blake: Done. Appreciate the fast turnaround.

title: #sales: 1770145503
preview:
Maya Ortiz: hey folks — quick customer-facing Q. how do i explain “billed tokens” vs “raw tokens” in a way that doesn’t sound like we’re hiding the ball? customer is looking at their app-side token counts vs our usage export.

Maya Ortiz: also how do i describe caching? they saw a day where their prompt tokens were huge but the invoice was lower and they’re suspicious.

Jordan Blake: following. this comes up weekly. would love a crisp 2–3 sentence answer + a longer version for the follow-up email.

Soojin Lee: +1. we should standardize the language. Maya, is this for a self-serve customer or in procurement mode?

Maya Ortiz: sales-led renewal convo (they’re technical). they use our OpenAI-compatible endpoint + streaming.

Nikhil Sharma: ok, here’s the framing that’s been working:
- “Raw tokens” = the tokens we *observe* for the request/response using our tokenizer (prompt + completion). It’s what you’d expect if you added up what was sent/received.
- “Billed tokens” = the tokens we charge for after applying billing rules that prevent double-charging (retries/idempotency) and account for cache hits.

Nikhil Sharma: key point: we show raw for transparency, but invoices are based on billed.

Nadia Rahman: adding a bit more detail (customer-safe):
- Streaming: we do NOT bill per chunk. We bill once per request based on the final aggregated token counts.
- Retries/fallback: if our platform retries internally (e.g., transient infra issue, fallback routing), we only bill the successful completion once. Failed attempts are not billed.

Nadia Rahman: caching language (keep it simple):
- When prompt caching is enabled and the request shares a prefix with previous prompts, the model can reuse cached KV state.
- Those cached prefix tokens may be billed at a reduced rate (or not billed depending on plan/config), which is why you can see raw prompt tokens high but billed tokens lower.

Cole Summers: +1 and important nuance: “raw” isn’t what the GPU necessarily computed in full if cache hit; it’s the logical token count of the text. that’s why raw is stable for auditability, and billed reflects the economic lever.

Priya Desai: from Support perspective, we’ve used this one-liner:
“Raw tokens reflect the size of your inputs/outputs; billed tokens reflect what you’re charged after platform optimizations (like cache reuse) and safeguards (like not double-counting retries).”

Maya Ortiz: this is good. do we have an example i can paste?

Nikhil Sharma: example you can use (numbers illustrative):
“Example: You send a 10,000-token prompt where the first 8,000 tokens match a previously cached prefix. Raw prompt tokens will still show ~10,000 for that request, but billed prompt tokens may reflect only the non-cached portion (or discounted cached portion), so your invoice can be lower while output quality stays the same.”

Jordan Blake: can we avoid committing to “not billed” vs “discounted” in writing? depends on plan right?

Nadia Rahman: yes. recommended phrasing:
- “cache-hit tokens may be discounted” (safe)
- “your invoice uses billed_tokens, which reflect cache discounts where applicable”

Nadia Rahman: also if they ask “why doesn’t my client-side tokenizer match exactly?” we can say:
- tokenizers differ by model/version
- we use the tokenizer associated with the model deployed in Redwood
- we expose the counts we use for billing in the usage API/export

Cole Summers: and if they’re comparing against OpenAI usage objects: we’re aligning semantics on the compat endpoints. the near-term answer: “use Redwood’s billed_tokens field for invoice reconciliation.”

Soojin Lee: here’s a customer-ready blurb (short):
“Redwood reports both raw and billed token counts. Raw tokens are the observed prompt/completion token totals for transparency. Billed tokens are what your invoice is based on after applying billing rules (e.g., not double-counting retries) and any cache-hit discounts when prompt caching is in effect.”

Soojin Lee: longer follow-up (email-friendly):
“Raw tokens reflect the logical size of the text sent and generated. Billed tokens reflect how Redwood charges for a request after applying platform safeguards and optimizations: (1) internal retries/failovers are deduped so you aren’t charged multiple times for the same request, (2) streaming responses are billed once based on the final aggregated output, and (3) when prompt caching is enabled, tokens served from cache may be discounted because they don’t require full recomputation. For invoice reconciliation, please rely on the billed token fields in the Redwood usage export/API, which match invoiced usage.”

Maya Ortiz: perfect. last Q: if they ask “how can i verify cache is happening?”

Cole Summers: safest answer today: “we expose cache-hit attribution in the usage export/API fields (rolling out) and can provide a short export for a time window.” Don’t promise exact per-request cache math beyond what we publish.

Nikhil Sharma: + we can offer: “We can compare a before/after window once caching is enabled and show the billed-vs-raw gap increasing as cache hit rate increases.”

Jordan Blake: cool. Maya, use Soojin’s blurb + the example. if procurement asks for contract language, route to Legal playbook.

Maya Ortiz: got it. thanks all — sending now.

Priya Desai: if they come back with “we think we’re double charged”, tag Support + we’ll pull the recon export. (we’ve had a few retry-related false positives recently but we have fixes landing.)

title: #sales: 1770012345
preview:
Aria Chen: Quick sync - enterprise wants a do-able plan for air-gapped POC: limited transfer (4GB/day), signed artifacts, proof-of-provenance, and a predictable hotfix cadence. They asked for written runbook and PHI-safe SBOM.
Samir Patel: Noted. Do they want chunked bundles or full images? They said they can't do reboots during trading hours.
Nina Lopez: Chunked bundles only. Recommend file-level deltas + a lightweight applyer that supports resume. No reboot for most agent/model weight updates.
Ben Carter: PS estimate: we'll need to help them build the USB workflow and initial validation. Recommend two remote sessions + one onsite day for handoff. Billable item.
Aria Chen: Security asks: HSM-backed signing or equivalent customer-side verification, detached signatures, and tamper-evident manifests. Also offline audit logs for bundle application.
Samir Patel: HSM integration is a custom PS scope. Estimate 2 engineer-weeks to add signing hooks + automated SBOM generation per-customer.
Nina Lopez: For SBOM, we'll provide SPDX JSON and a plain-text summary. Include verification instructions like: `sha256sum parts/* | sort > manifest.sha256` and `openssl dgst -sha256 -verify pubkey.pem -signature bundle.sig`.
QA Bot: ci-bot: automated test run for chunked-applyer passed (unit + e2e). artifact: 1770012345-chunked-applyer-v1.tar.gz
Ben Carter: Operational constraints: they have strict change-freeze windows 00:00-04:00 UTC only twice a month. We'll need an agreed classification: security-critical vs noncritical.
Samir Patel: Agree. Propose policy: quarterly feature upgrades, monthly security advisories, emergency patches on-request (SLA + premium).
Aria Chen: Transfer size problem: If a hotfix >4GB we should split into numbered chunks with a manifest and integrity checks. The applyer must support `--verify-only` and `--dry-run`.
Nina Lopez: We should avoid block-deltas across compressed artifacts. Use file-level diffs (rsync-friendly) and signed tar chunks. Example manifest snippet: `{
  "chunks": ["0001.tar.gz","0002.tar.gz"],

}`

title: #sales: 1769537745
preview:
Markus Klein: anyone have an approved “Redwood Private deployment security appendix” we can attach to an RFP? Helix Pharma is asking for a dedicated section that spells out controls + shared responsibility (VPC/on‑prem). Our current RFP library is mostly Hosted/Dedicated language.

Stephanie Nguyen: don’t think we have a standalone appendix yet. What’s the ask specifically? (network isolation / customer-managed keys / logging / etc)

Markus Klein: yep. they want (1) architecture/data flow summary for Private, (2) boundary of responsibility (Redwood vs customer), (3) access controls (SSO/RBAC), (4) audit logging + export, (5) vuln mgmt/patching expectations, (6) incident response + how we do evidence (SOC2 report under NDA). They explicitly said “appendix preferred vs answers sprinkled throughout.”

Jordan Blake: +1 we’ve hit this a few times lately. would be great to standardize as an addendum/appendix block in the library.

Hannah Schmitt: EMEA deals too. Nordbank’s questionnaire basically assumed Private = “fully customer controlled” and we had to do a bunch of one-off explanations.

Naomi Feldman: agreed we should add a Private appendix section to the RFP response library (not a new product promise). Colin + Fatima: can you draft the technical bullets + boundaries? Olga can sanity check the compliance phrasing.

Colin O'Donnell: can do. For clarity: “Redwood Private” is either (a) customer VPC with private control plane connectivity, or (b) on‑prem package for regulated/air‑gapped. We need to be careful to keep it generic + not overfit to one implementation.

Fatima Noor: I’ll help draft, esp the regulated/on‑prem expectations. We should include the “who patches what” section (customer owns OS/hypervisor if on‑prem; Redwood owns app/runtime components we ship, per contract), but keep it high level.

Olga Petrov: please also include a short “evidence availability” paragraph + link to the evidence request process (NDA required for SOC 2 report). And avoid ISO language beyond “alignment/mapping” per our do-not-say guidance.

Marcus Lin: +1 on boundaries. Also call out: no inbound access by default; any break-glass access is time-bound + customer-approved + logged. For VPC, network paths are constrained to approved endpoints; for on‑prem, customer controls perimeter.

Avery Johnson: what do we say re: “data never leaves region” in Private? I keep getting asked.

Naomi Feldman: we should stick to approved wording: residency depends on selected deployment region + customer-managed infra in Private. Don’t claim “never leaves” without specifics. For VPC/on‑prem, processing occurs in the customer environment, but support/telemetry may be configured/limited—needs the standard caveats.

Camila Reyes: once drafted, please send to me for messaging pass. We should keep it consistent with our security pillars (shared responsibility, isolation, encryption, auditability) + keep it “competitive-safe.”

Markus Klein: timeline: Helix wants this by Thurs EOD. Even a “v0.1” we can attach with disclaimer “subject to contract/SSOW” would help.

Olga Petrov: we can do a v0.1 as long as it’s approved language. No “subject to roadmap” / no features not GA. Add a footer like “This appendix describes current capabilities as of [date].”

Colin O'Donnell: plan: I’ll draft an appendix section today in the RFP library doc with headings:
- Overview (Private = VPC/on‑prem options)
- Data flow + isolation (high level)
- Shared responsibility model (table-ish text)
- Identity & access (SAML SSO, SCIM where applicable, RBAC)
- Logging & audit (events, export options, retention options)
- Encryption & keys (at-rest/in-transit; KMS/HSM options by mode)
- Vulnerability management & patching (responsibilities)
- Incident response & notification (process, contractual)
- Compliance evidence (SOC 2 report request under NDA)

Fatima Noor: I’ll add language on regulated deployments: customer-controlled networking, options to disable/limit telemetry, and operational expectations (change windows, upgrade cadence) without hard commitments.

Stephanie Nguyen: perfect. once it’s in the library, we should also add a HubSpot snippet + point #sales to the canonical link so we don’t fork docs.

Jordan Blake: +1. Also add a note to the Confluence enablement kit index once published.

Marcus Lin: when you draft, ping me for a quick accuracy pass (esp the “break-glass access” wording + audit log fields). (reaction :thumbsup: from Markus Klein)

Naomi Feldman: action items:
- Colin + Fatima draft v0.1 appendix in RFP library today
- Marcus review for technical accuracy
- Olga review for compliance/evidence phrasing
- Camila final messaging pass
- Stephanie to coordinate publishing/linking once approved

Markus Klein: thanks all — I’ll tell Helix we’re sending an approved appendix tomorrow morning and keep them on track. (reaction :white_check_mark: from Stephanie Nguyen)

title: #sales: 1769411188
preview:
Avery Johnson: Getting the ISO question again in a questionnaire: “Are you ISO 27001 certified?” What’s our exact wording rn? I don’t want to accidentally imply cert.

Hannah Schmitt: +1. Nordbank asked last week and they were super picky about “compliant” vs “aligned”.

Lukas Meyer: Same in EMEA. If we say “ISO-aligned” they then ask for certificate number :grimacing:

Stephanie Nguyen: Can we get a canonical copy block we can paste into RFPs? Also where is the source of truth (claims matrix vs Confluence)?

Olga Petrov: Jumping in. Approved language (please use verbatim unless you get an exception):

Olga Petrov: 1) If asked “Are you ISO 27001 certified?”
Answer: “Redwood Inference is not currently ISO/IEC 27001 certified. We maintain a security program and information security controls that are aligned to widely adopted industry standards, and we can provide our SOC 2 Type II report under NDA as evidence of our control environment.”

Olga Petrov: 2) If asked “Are you ISO 27001 compliant?”
Answer: “We do not claim ISO/IEC 27001 compliance or certification. Upon request, we can discuss how our security controls map to ISO/IEC 27001 themes (e.g., access control, logging/monitoring, vulnerability management, incident response), and we can provide third-party audit evidence (SOC 2 Type II) under NDA.”

Olga Petrov: 3) If they ask for an “ISO attestation letter”
Answer: “We do not provide ISO attestation letters. We can provide our SOC 2 Type II report under NDA and respond to specific control questions.”

Olga Petrov: Hard “do not say”: “ISO compliant”, “ISO certified pending”, “ISO in progress”, “ISO-ready”, “meets ISO 27001 requirements”. Those have all created problems in redlines.

Avery Johnson: This is perfect. Is there a link I can cite internally so the AE team stops remixing it?

Olga Petrov: Yep. Claims matrix row is the source of truth for copy/paste wording:
- Security claims matrix (restricted): https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/RI-Security-Claims-Matrix (tab: “Compliance - ISO27001”)
- Confluence guidance (non-restricted): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/x/security-faq/what-we-do-and-dont-say#iso27001

Daniel Carter: Adding: if a customer insists on ISO 27001 as a hard requirement, route to GRC early. Sometimes they’ll accept SOC 2 Type II + mapping narrative, sometimes not. Don’t try to negotiate language in-email.

Hannah Schmitt: When they ask “aligned” they usually follow with “show me the mapping”. Are we okay to say we can map Annex A controls?

Olga Petrov: Careful on wording. Approved: “we can discuss how our controls map to ISO/IEC 27001 themes” (high-level). If you need an Annex A control-by-control mapping for an RFP, that’s a GRC deliverable and depends on NDA + timeline. Don’t promise it unilaterally.

Lukas Meyer: For EU banks they literally have a table with each Annex A control and want Yes/No. Should we just say “see SOC2 report” for those?

Daniel Carter: For those: respond with the ISO block Olga posted + “available under NDA: SOC 2 Type II report” + offer a follow-up call. If the RFP requires a strict Yes/No per control, flag it as an exception and we’ll provide alternative language where appropriate.

Naomi Feldman: +1 on not implying roadmap. We’ve had a few questionnaires where “aligned” got interpreted as “certification planned this year.” Please avoid any “in progress” / “working toward” phrasing.

Camila Reyes: From messaging side: we can also add a one-liner to reduce friction:
“While we are not ISO 27001 certified, we undergo independent third-party audits (SOC 2 Type II) and maintain a security program designed around common control expectations.”
This is consistent with the Security & Trust pillars doc.

Stephanie Nguyen: Can we get this into the RFP response library + HubSpot snippet so it’s not tribal knowledge?

Olga Petrov: Yes. Please open an INT request (or tag me) if you need a snippet added. The canonical text is what I pasted above.

Jordan Blake: Thanks all. Stephanie—let’s make sure the enablement kit index points to the claims matrix + this exact block. We’re losing time on rework every time.

Avery Johnson: Copying verbatim into the Nordbank response now. Thanks Olga/Daniel.

Hannah Schmitt: :thumbsup: bookmarking that Confluence link.

Lukas Meyer: :eyes: on getting the HubSpot snippet updated. I’ll ping Isabelle separately.

Daniel Carter: One more reminder: SOC 2 report request is NDA-gated. Don’t attach the report directly; use the evidence request playbook link and we’ll fulfill.

Olga Petrov: ^ yep. Evidence request playbook: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/x/compliance-evidence/soc2-customer-evidence-request-playbook

title: #sales: 1769380021
preview:
Stephanie Nguyen: Starting a running list of marketplace procurement objections/questions I’m hearing this week. Please drop yours + any good canned responses. Goal is to map these into the updated customer-facing Marketplace FAQ + internal enablement one-pager.

Stephanie Nguyen: Top 5 from my calls:
1) “Can we apply our AWS EDP / commits if we buy via Marketplace?”
2) “Can you do a private offer with net terms / annual commit?”
3) “What exactly do we get when we click Subscribe? Do we get an org automatically or do we need SE help?”
4) “We need SOC 2 + encryption/KMS + audit logs. Where’s the packet? Is it public?”
5) “We have multiple AWS accounts / multiple projects. Can we link multiple subscriptions to one Redwood org?”

Markus Klein: +1 on (3). Also getting “What are the support SLAs for Marketplace customers? Is it the same as direct?” and “Do we get a trial/credits through Marketplace or only direct?”

Lukas Meyer: EMEA angle: “We need GCP Marketplace (not AWS). Can you guarantee data residency in EU regions?” and “Can we turn off data retention / set custom retention?”

Isabelle Laurent: Adding ops/process one: “If we buy in Marketplace, does it still count toward our internal spend tracking? How does invoicing look (AWS invoice vs Redwood invoice)?”

Soojin Lee: For mapping to assets, here are the drafts we should point to (I’ll update links once final URLs are locked):
- Customer-facing FAQ draft (GDrive): /shared_drives/go-to-market/customer-facing-marketplace-faq-draft
- Procurement one-pager (Confluence): /sales-enablement/pitch-decks/marketplace-procurement-one-pager
- Security packet index (Confluence): /security-and-compliance/compliance-evidence/marketplace-security-documentation-pack
- Data handling/retention (Confluence): /security-and-compliance/data-residency-and-retention/marketplace-data-handling-and-retention

Jordan Blake: Please make sure we’re consistent on EDP/commits language. Don’t promise “EDP automatically applies” — depends on customer’s AWS agreement. We should say “Marketplace purchases may count toward AWS commitments depending on your AWS contract; confirm with your AWS rep.”

Ben Carter: I can take (3) and (5) and convert into FAQ entries w/ the new onboarding flow language. Need latest from console team on entitlement propagation timing.

Rachel Kim: From onboarding calls: “How long does entitlement take after Subscribe?” We should give a realistic range + troubleshooting steps (and a clear escalation path). Also “We already have a Redwood org. Can we attach the marketplace subscription to it without creating a new org?”

Dev Patel: Support perspective:
- Expect tickets: provisioning delay, org linking confusion, billing dimension mismatch.
- We should add a line: if entitlement hasn’t shown up after X minutes, check Y, then open a ticket with marketplace subscription ID.
- Also clarify who provides L1 support (Redwood) vs what must go to AWS/GCP support.

Stephanie Nguyen: Awesome. I’ll start mapping Qs -> recommended response + asset.

Stephanie Nguyen: 1) “Does AWS EDP/commit apply?”
Recommended response: “Marketplace purchases may count toward your AWS commitment depending on your AWS agreement. We can provide the product/offer details; your AWS rep can confirm how it applies to your EDP.”
Asset: Procurement one-pager (section: ‘When Marketplace helps’)
Owner: Soojin to confirm final wording.

Soojin Lee: Yep. I’ll add a “do/don’t” snippet in the enablement doc. (reaction :thumbsup: from Jordan Blake)

Stephanie Nguyen: 2) “Private offer / net terms / annual commit?”
Recommended response: “Yes — for Dedicated/Private and larger Hosted commitments we can do private offers in AWS/GCP Marketplace. Standard terms/discount guardrails apply; we’ll loop in Partnerships + Finance for approvals.”
Asset: internal AWS private offer notes + Procurement one-pager

Isabelle Laurent: Please also capture what we need from the rep to initiate: AWS account ID, payer, marketplace seller ID (if applicable), desired start date, term, and SKU. A bunch of deals stall because we’re missing basic info.

Markus Klein: 3) “What happens after Subscribe?”
Good simplified answer (what I’ve been saying): “Subscribe in Marketplace → you’ll be redirected to Redwood Console to sign in/create an account → we verify entitlement → you either create a new org or link to an existing org → create first API key → run a quickstart request.”
We should put this exact flow in the FAQ and in the listing ‘How it works’ section.

Ben Carter: +1. I’ll add explicit callouts:
- If entitlement isn’t available yet: wait/retry + check org email domain
- If already have org: choose ‘Link existing org’
- If multiple subscriptions: we support linking multiple entitlements to one org (with admin controls)

Rachel Kim: Can we be specific on entitlement timing? I’ve seen ~2–10 min typical, but sometimes 30+ min edge cases.

Dev Patel: Suggest wording: “Typically a few minutes; in rare cases up to ~30 minutes. If it’s been >30 minutes, contact support with your subscription ID.” Keeps it honest.

Stephanie Nguyen: 4) “Security packet / SOC2 / encryption / audit logs. Is it public?”
Recommended response: “We have a marketplace-ready security overview covering SOC 2 posture, encryption in transit/at rest, KMS options (where applicable), audit logs, retention, and shared responsibility. Some evidence is gated; we can share under NDA via the trust portal/process.”
Asset: Security documentation pack index + questionnaire responses draft

Soojin Lee: Please avoid “SOC2 certified” language. Use “SOC 2 Type II report available under NDA” (or whatever Security approved). Marcus/Olga have been strict about this.

Lukas Meyer: 5) “Data residency / EU region?”
Recommended response: “Hosted supports region selection for supported regions; Dedicated/Private can be deployed in customer-selected regions/VPC/on-prem. We’ll confirm availability for your required geography.”
Asset: Data handling & residency page

Jordan Blake: Keep it simple but don’t overcommit on “guarantees” unless it’s in the SKU/terms. For regulated workloads, steer to Dedicated/Private.

Isabelle Laurent: 6) “Invoicing / who bills us?”
Answer: “AWS/GCP invoices you via Marketplace; Redwood does not send a separate invoice for marketplace usage (except as needed for support/PS per contract).”
Asset: revrec/invoicing notes (internal) + customer FAQ section.

Markus Klein: 7) “Support SLAs?”
Answer: “Support depends on the SKU/support tier purchased. Marketplace subscription includes standard support; enterprise support/SLA is available via Dedicated/Private offers (often via private offer).”
We should make sure the listing doesn’t imply 24/7 for everyone.

Dev Patel: +1. Also clarify where to open tickets. “Use Redwood Support portal/email; include marketplace subscription ID and org ID.” If it’s a marketplace portal issue (can’t subscribe), that’s AWS/GCP.

Ben Carter: 8) “Trial/credits through Marketplace?”
What’s the official stance? I’ve seen inconsistent answers.

Stephanie Nguyen: quick PSA since this came up twice today — SOC 2 report requests should *not* be handled via email attachments / “can you just send it to me” 🙏

Stephanie Nguyen: ✅ Canonical process (SOC 2 + evidence):
1) Confirm NDA is in place (or get one executed). If they don’t have one → loop Legal via the deal room thread, don’t improvise language.
2) Open an evidence request using the playbook + ticketing flow (so GRC can track SLA + what we actually shared)
3) Only send the customer the approved link/secure share path once GRC confirms NDA + packages the artifact.

Stephanie Nguyen: Confluence playbook here (bookmark it): https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/Security/soc2-customer-evidence-request-playbook

Stephanie Nguyen: TL;DR: if customer says “need SOC2 by Friday” → do NOT DM a PDF around. Create the request + tag #security-and-compliance / @Olga Petrov / @Daniel Carter if truly urgent.

Avery Johnson: +1, had a prospect asking me to forward “whatever you sent Acme” 😬 glad there’s a clean process.

Hannah Schmitt: for NDA — do we have a default form we can send, or always customer paper?

Daniel Carter: typically customer paper, but we can provide ours if needed. Either way: route through Legal + deal channel. Please don’t accept NDA terms in email.

Olga Petrov: also reminder: SOC 2 report is shared *under NDA only* and via controlled distribution. We track which accounts received which version.

Markus Klein: on the request intake — should SEs file it or AEs?

Olga Petrov: either is fine, but please make sure the request includes:
- account name + domain
- due date
- what they’re asking for (SOC2 report vs bridge letter vs pen test summary etc)
- deployment mode (Hosted/Dedicated/Private) + any specific control gaps they’re worried about

Jordan Blake: and pls cite the current kit version in the deal notes so we don’t have “random PDF from last year” floating around.

Rachel Kim: +1 from CS side — once it’s shared, please drop the evidence request link in the account channel so we can reference later (renewals always re-ask)

Security GRC Bot: Reminder: SOC 2 artifacts are restricted. If you need access to the evidence folder or can’t locate the request form, follow the playbook: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/display/Security/soc2-customer-evidence-request-playbook

Stephanie Nguyen: thanks all. if anyone sees SOC2 PDF being forwarded around, call it out (kindly) + point back here. :thumbsup:
